title: The Village Wife
content:
ï¿½Ouse-keeper sent tha my lass, fur New Squire coomï¿½d last night.
Butter anï¿½ heggsï¿½yisï¿½yis. Iï¿½ll goï¿½ wiï¿½ tha back: all right;
Butter I warrants be prime, anï¿½ I warrants the heggs be as well,
Hafe a pint oï¿½ milk runs out when ya breï¿½ks the shell.

II.
Sit thysen down fur a bit: hev a glass oï¿½ cowslip wine!
I liked the owd Squire anï¿½ ï¿½is gells as thaw they was gells oï¿½ mine,
Fur then we was all es one, the Squire anï¿½ ï¿½is darters anï¿½ me,
Hall but Miss Annie, the heldest, I niver not took to she:
But Nelly, the last of the cletch,2 I liked ï¿½er the fust on ï¿½em all,
Fur hoffens we talkt oï¿½ my darter es died oï¿½ the fever at fall:
Anï¿½ I thowt ï¿½twur the will oï¿½ the Lord, but Miss Annie she said it wur draï¿½ins,
Fur she hednï¿½t naw coomfut in ï¿½er, anï¿½ arnï¿½d naw thanks fur ï¿½er paï¿½ins.
Eh! thebbe all wiï¿½ the Lord my childer, I hanï¿½t gotten none!
Sa new squireï¿½s coomï¿½d wiï¿½ ï¿½is taï¿½il in ï¿½is ï¿½and, anï¿½ owd Squireï¿½s gone.

III.
Fur ï¿½staï¿½te be iï¿½ taï¿½il, my lass: tha dosnï¿½ knaw what that be?
But I knaws the law, I does, for the lawyer ha towd it me.
ï¿½When theerï¿½s naw ï¿½ead to a ï¿½Ouse by the fault oï¿½ that ere maï¿½leï¿½
The gells they counts fur nowt, and the next un he taï¿½kes the taï¿½il.ï¿½

IV.
What be the next un like? can tha tell ony harm on ï¿½im lass?ï¿½
Naï¿½y sit downï¿½naw ï¿½urryï¿½sa cowd!ï¿½hev another glass!
Straï¿½nge anï¿½ cowd fur the time! we may happen a fall oï¿½ snawï¿½
Not es I cares fur to hear ony harm, but I likes to knaw.
Anï¿½ I ï¿½oï¿½ps es ï¿½e beï¿½nt booï¿½klarnï¿½d: but ï¿½e dosnï¿½ not coom froï¿½ the shere;
Weï¿½d anew oï¿½ that wiï¿½ the Squire, anï¿½ we haï¿½tes booï¿½klarninï¿½ ere.

V.
Fur Squire wur a Varsity scholard, anï¿½ niver lookt arter the landï¿½
Whoï¿½ts or tonups or taï¿½tesï¿½ï¿½e ï¿½ed hallus a booï¿½k iï¿½ ï¿½is ï¿½and,
Hallus aloï¿½n wiï¿½ ï¿½is booï¿½ks, thaw nigh upoï¿½ seventy year.
Anï¿½ booï¿½ks, whatï¿½s booï¿½ks? thou knaws thebbe naither ï¿½ere nor theer.

VI.
Anï¿½ the gells, they hednï¿½t naw taï¿½ils, anï¿½ the lawyer he towd it me
That ï¿½is taï¿½il were soï¿½ tied up es he couldnï¿½t cut down a tree!
ï¿½Drat the trees,ï¿½ says I, to be sewer I haï¿½tes ï¿½em, my lass,
Fur we puts the muck oï¿½ the land anï¿½ they sucks the muck froï¿½ the grass.

Vll.
Anï¿½ Squire wur hallus a-smilinï¿½, anï¿½ gied to the tramps goinï¿½ byï¿½
Anï¿½ all oï¿½ the wust iï¿½ the parishï¿½wiï¿½ hoffens a drop in ï¿½is eye.
Anï¿½ ivry darter oï¿½ Squireï¿½s hed her awn ridin-erse to ï¿½ersen,
Anï¿½ they rampaged about wiï¿½ their grooms, anï¿½ was ï¿½untenï¿½ arter the men,
Anï¿½ hallus a-dallackt3 anï¿½ dizenï¿½d out, anï¿½ a-buyinï¿½ new cloï¿½thes,
While ï¿½e sit like a greï¿½t glimmer-gowk4 wiï¿½ ï¿½is glasses athurt ï¿½is noï¿½se,
Anï¿½ ï¿½is noï¿½se sa grufted wiï¿½ snuff es it couldnï¿½t be scroobï¿½d awaï¿½y,
Fur atween ï¿½is reï¿½dinï¿½ anï¿½ writinï¿½ ï¿½e sniff: up a box in a daï¿½y,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e niver runnï¿½d arter the fox, nor arter the birds wiï¿½ ï¿½is gun,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e niver not shot one ï¿½are, but ï¿½e leï¿½ved it to Charlie ï¿½is son,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e niver not fishï¿½d ï¿½is awn ponds, but Charlie ï¿½e cotchï¿½d the pike,
For ï¿½e warnï¿½t not burn to the land, anï¿½ ï¿½e didnï¿½t take kind to it like;
But I eï¿½rs es ï¿½eï¿½d gie fur a howry5 owd book thutty pound anï¿½ moor,
Anï¿½ ï¿½eï¿½d wrote an owd book, his awn sen, sa I knawï¿½d esï¿½eï¿½d coom to be poor;
Anï¿½ ï¿½e giedï¿½I be fearï¿½d fur to tell tha ï¿½ow muchï¿½fur an owd scratted stoï¿½n,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e diggï¿½d up a loomp iï¿½ the land anï¿½ ï¿½e got a brown pot anï¿½ a boï¿½n,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e bowt owd money, es wouldnï¿½t goï¿½, wiï¿½ good gowd oï¿½ the Queen,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e bowt little statutes all-naï¿½kt anï¿½ which was a shaï¿½me to be seen;
But ï¿½e niver looï¿½kt ower a bill, nor ï¿½e niver not seed to owt,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e niver knawd nowt but booï¿½ks, anï¿½ booï¿½ks, as thou knaws, beï¿½nt nowt.

VIII.
But owd Squireï¿½s laï¿½dy es long es she lived she kep ï¿½em all clear,
Thaw es long es she lived I niver hed none of ï¿½er darters ï¿½ere;
Burt arter she died we was all es one, the childer anï¿½ me,
Anï¿½ sarvints runnï¿½d in anï¿½ out, anï¿½ offens we hed ï¿½em to tea.
Lawk! ï¿½ow I laughï¿½d when the lasses ï¿½ud talk oï¿½ their Missisï¿½s waï¿½ys,
Anï¿½ the Missisis talkï¿½d oï¿½ the lasses.ï¿½Iï¿½ll tell tha some oï¿½ these daï¿½ys.
Hoï¿½nly Miss Annie were saw stuck oop, like ï¿½er mother afoorï¿½
ï¿½Er anï¿½ ï¿½er blessed darterï¿½they niver derkenï¿½d my door.

IX.
Anï¿½ Squire ï¿½e smiled anï¿½ ï¿½e smiled till ï¿½eï¿½d gotten a fright at last,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e calls fur ï¿½is son, fur the ï¿½turneyï¿½s letters they follerï¿½d sa fast;
But Squire wur afearï¿½d oï¿½ ï¿½is son, anï¿½ ï¿½e says to ï¿½im, meek as a mouse,
ï¿½Lad, thou mun cut off thy taï¿½il, or the gells ï¿½ull goï¿½ to the ï¿½Ouse,
Fur I finds es I be that iï¿½ debt, es I ï¿½oaps es thouï¿½ll ï¿½elp me a bit,
Anï¿½ if thouï¿½ll ï¿½gree to cut off thy taï¿½il I may saï¿½ve mysen yit.ï¿½

X.
But Charlie ï¿½e sets back ï¿½is ears, anï¿½ ï¿½e swears, anï¿½ ï¿½e says to ï¿½im ï¿½Noa.
Iï¿½ve gotten the ï¿½staï¿½te by the taï¿½il anï¿½ be dangï¿½d if I iver let goa!
Coom! coom! feyther,ï¿½ ï¿½e says, ï¿½why shouldnï¿½t thy booï¿½ks be sowd?
I hears es soom oï¿½ thy booï¿½ks mebbe worth their weight iï¿½ gowd.ï¿½

XI.
Heï¿½ps anï¿½ heï¿½ps oï¿½ booï¿½ks, I haï¿½ seeï¿½d ï¿½em, belongï¿½d to the Squire,
But the lasses ï¿½ed teï¿½rd out leï¿½ves iï¿½ the middle to kindle the fire;
Sa moï¿½st on ï¿½is owd big booï¿½ks fetchï¿½d nigh to nowt at the saï¿½le,
And Squire were at Charlie agean to git ï¿½im to cut off ï¿½is taï¿½il.

XII.
Ya wouldnï¿½t find Charlieï¿½s likesï¿½ï¿½e were that outdacious at ï¿½oam,
Not thaw ya went fur to raï¿½ke out Hell wiï¿½ a small-tooth coambï¿½
Droonk wiï¿½ the Quolotyï¿½s wine, anï¿½ droonk wiï¿½ the farmerï¿½s a 8;le,
Mad wiï¿½ the lasses anï¿½ allï¿½anï¿½ ï¿½e wouldnï¿½t cut off the taï¿½il.

XIII.
Thouï¿½s coomï¿½d oop by the beck; and a thurn be a-grawinï¿½ theer,
I niver ha seed it sa white wiï¿½ the Maï¿½y es I seeï¿½d it to-yearï¿½
Theerabouts Charlie joomptï¿½and it gied me a scare tother night,
Fur I thowt it wur Charlieï¿½s ghoï¿½st iï¿½ the derk, fur it looï¿½kt sa white.
ï¿½Billy,ï¿½ says ï¿½e, ï¿½hev a joomp!ï¿½ï¿½thaw the banks oï¿½ the beck be sa high,
Fur he caï¿½d ï¿½is ï¿½erse Billy-rough-un, thaw niver a hair wur awry;
But Billy fell bakkuds oï¿½ Charlie, anï¿½ Charlie ï¿½e brok ï¿½is neck,
Sa theer wur a hend oï¿½ the taï¿½il, fur ï¿½e lost ï¿½is taï¿½il iï¿½ the beck.

XIV.
Sa ï¿½is taï¿½il wur lost anï¿½ ï¿½is booï¿½ks wur gone anï¿½ ï¿½is boy wur deï¿½d,
Anï¿½ Squire ï¿½e smiled anï¿½ ï¿½e smiled, but ï¿½e niver not lift oop ï¿½is ï¿½eï¿½d:
Hallus a soft un Squire! anï¿½ ï¿½e smiled, fur ï¿½e hednï¿½t naw friend,
Sa feyther anï¿½ son was buried togither, anï¿½ this wur the hend.

XV.
Anï¿½ Parson as hesnï¿½t the call, nor the mooney, but hes the pride,
ï¿½E reads of a sewer anï¿½ sartan ï¿½oï¿½p oï¿½ the tother side;
But I beï¿½nt that sewer es the Lord, how-siver they praï¿½yï¿½d anï¿½ praï¿½yï¿½d,
Lets them inter ï¿½eaven eï¿½sy es leï¿½ves their debts to be paï¿½id.
Siver the mouï¿½ds rattled down upoï¿½ poor owd Squire iï¿½ the wood,
Anï¿½ I cried along wiï¿½ the gells, fur they weï¿½nt niver coons to naw good.

XVI.
Fur Molly the long un she walkt awaï¿½y wiï¿½ a hofficer lad,
Anï¿½ nawbody ï¿½eï¿½rd on ï¿½er sin, sa oï¿½ coorse she be gone to the bad!
Anï¿½ Lucy wur laï¿½me oï¿½ one leg, sweet-ï¿½arts she niver ï¿½ed noneï¿½
Straï¿½nge anï¿½ unheppen6 Miss Lucy! we naï¿½med her ï¿½ Dot anï¿½ gaw one!ï¿½
Anï¿½ Hetty wur weak iï¿½ the hattics, wiï¿½out ony harm iï¿½ the legs,
Anï¿½ the fever ï¿½ed baï¿½ked Jinnyï¿½s ï¿½eï¿½d as bald as one oï¿½ them heggs,
Anï¿½ Nelly wur up froï¿½ the craï¿½dle as big iï¿½ the mouth as a cow,
Anï¿½ saw she mun hammergrate,7 lass, of she weï¿½nt git a maï¿½te onyhow!
Anï¿½ es for Miss Annie es callï¿½d me afoor my awn foï¿½lks to my faï¿½ce
ï¿½A hignorant village wife as ï¿½ud hev to be larnï¿½d her awn plaï¿½ce,ï¿½
Hes fur Miss Hannie the heldest hes now be a-grawinï¿½ sa howd,
I knaws that mooch oï¿½ sheï¿½, es it beï¿½nt not fit to be towd!

XVII.
Sa I didnï¿½t not taï¿½ke it kindly ov owd Miss Annie to saï¿½y
Es I should be talkin ageï¿½n ï¿½em, es soon es they went awaï¿½y,
Fur, lawks! ï¿½ow I cried when they went, anï¿½ our Nelly she gied me ï¿½er ï¿½and,
Fur Iï¿½d ha done owt for the Squire anï¿½ ï¿½is gells es belongï¿½d to the land;
Booï¿½ks, es I said afoor, thebbe neyther ï¿½ere nor theer!
But I sarved ï¿½em wiï¿½ butter anï¿½ heggs fur huppuds oï¿½ twenty year.

XVIII.
Anï¿½ they hallus paï¿½id what I haxï¿½d, sa I hallus dealï¿½d wiï¿½ the Hall,
Anï¿½ they knawï¿½d what butter wur, anï¿½ they knawï¿½d what a hegg wur anï¿½ all;
Hugger-mugger they lived, but they wasnï¿½t that eï¿½sy to pleï¿½se,
Till I gied ï¿½em Hinjian curn, anï¿½ they laï¿½id big heggs es tha seeas;
Anï¿½ I niver puts saï¿½me8 iï¿½ my butter, they does it at Willisï¿½s farm,
Taï¿½ste another drop oï¿½ the wineï¿½tweï¿½nt do tha naw harm.

XIX.
Sa new Squireï¿½s coomï¿½d wiï¿½ ï¿½is taï¿½il in ï¿½is ï¿½and, anï¿½ owd Squireï¿½s gone;
I heard ï¿½im a roomlinï¿½ by, but arter my nightcap wur on;
Sa I hanï¿½t clapt eyes on ï¿½im yit, fur he coomï¿½d last night sa laï¿½teï¿½
Pluksh! ! ! 9 the hens iï¿½ the peï¿½s! why didnï¿½t tha hesp the gaï¿½te?
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
